meta.title: Cronologia — Glossary
meta.subtitle: Cited definitions of contested terms
meta.description: The shared glossary of the Cronologia projects: short, source-referenced definitions of the canonical, theological and political vocabulary the chronologies rely on — one stable anchor per term, so project pages link instead of re-explaining.
meta.dataQualityNote: Definitions only, each cited like any other fact in the Cronologia family. Arguments about these terms stay in the projects' own disambiguation sections, which link here. Corrections against primary sources are welcome.

terms.definition: A penalty in Catholic canon law that is incurred automatically ('by the sentence already passed') at the moment the offense is committed, without a judgment being pronounced — as opposed to a ferendae sententiae penalty, which binds only after being imposed by a superior or court (can. 1314). The 1988 and 2026 excommunications in the SSPX story were declared as latae sententiae: the declarations state that the penalty had already been incurred by the act.

The gravest canonical censure: the excommunicated person is forbidden to celebrate or receive the sacraments and to exercise ecclesiastical offices or functions (can. 1331). Canonists stress it is a medicinal penalty aimed at repentance, not a declaration that someone is 'expelled from the Church' — a nuance both sides of the traditionalist disputes invoke.

A censure that forbids a cleric the exercise of the power of orders — celebrating Mass, administering sacraments (can. 1333). Marcel Lefebvre was suspended a divinis in July 1976 after ordaining priests against an explicit prohibition; Ernesto Cardenal was suspended in 1984 for holding government office, a suspension lifted by Francis in 2019.

In the 1983 Code's definition, 'the refusal of submission to the Supreme Pontiff or of communion with the members of the Church subject to him' (can. 751). Whether a given group is 'in schism' is often exactly what is disputed: the Holy See called the 1988 Écône consecrations 'a schismatic act' while later officials described the SSPX as not formally schismatic — and the 2026 decree changed that language again. Statements must be dated and attributed.

The Holy See's characterization of the SSPX's position between 1975 and 2026: the Society possessed 'no canonical status in the Church' and its ministers 'do not legitimately exercise any ministry' — Benedict XVI's 2009 wording — while officials in the same era distinguished this irregular condition from formal schism. A status label, not a doctrinal verdict; always date it.

A papal legal document issued 'on his own initiative', not in response to a request. Several hinge documents of these chronologies are motu proprio: Ecclesia Dei (1988), Summorum Pontificum (2007), Traditionis Custodes (2021).

An associational form of canon law under which the SSPX was erected in 1970 by Bishop Charrière of Lausanne, Geneva and Fribourg, on an ad experimentum basis — the diocesan approval whose 1975 withdrawal (disputed by the Society) opened the canonical conflict.

The position that the See of Peter is vacant — that the men elected as pope since Vatican II (the cut-off varies by group) are not true popes. Distinct from the SSPX's position, which recognizes the reigning pope while resisting parts of Vatican II; groups such as the SSPV milieu and the CMRI are sedevacantist. First systematically published by Fr. Joaquín Sáenz y Arriaga in the early 1970s.

The refined vacant-see position formulated by the Dominican theologian Michel-Louis Guérard des Lauriers (a former Écône professor) in the Cahiers de Cassiciacum (1979–81): the occupant of the see is pope 'materially' but not 'formally' — retaining the designation but not the authority. Followed today by institutes descending from his Thục-line consecration (1981).

Going beyond declaring the see vacant to electing one's own pope. The Palmarian Church of El Palmar de Troya (Spain) is the best-known case: after the 1976 Thục consecrations, Clemente Domínguez proclaimed himself 'Pope Gregory XVII' in 1978. No connection to the SSPX, which the Cronologia genealogies keep visually separate.

terms.variants: Congregation (now Dicastery) for the Doctrine of the Faith; historically the Holy Office
terms.definition: The Vatican's doctrinal watchdog: the Congregation for the Doctrine of the Faith, renamed Dicastery (DDF) in the 2022 curial reform. Author of the documents both chronologies pivot on — Libertatis Nuntius (1984) and Libertatis Conscientia (1986), the Boff and Sobrino notifications, and the 2026 SSPX decree.

A public doctrinal caution issued by the CDF/DDF about specific works or propositions of an author — less than a condemnation of the person. Leonardo Boff (1985) received a Notification followed by an imposed silence; Jon Sobrino (2006/07) a Notification without silencing; Gustavo Gutiérrez was scrutinized but never sanctioned. The differences matter: 'the Vatican condemned liberation theology' flattens three distinct outcomes.

Grassroots 'base ecclesial communities' — small neighborhood groups reading the Gospel in connection with daily life — strongest in Brazil, where they carried liberation theology's pastoral practice and, per attributed accounts, fed cadres into the Workers' Party in the 1980s. Estimates of their peak numbers vary widely between sources.

The formula at the heart of Latin American liberation theology, consolidated by the CELAM conferences of Medellín (1968) and Puebla (1979) and since absorbed into papal teaching. Its interpretation — pastoral priority vs political program — is precisely what the 1980s disputes were about.

Liberation theology's methodological keyword: theology as 'critical reflection on praxis' (Gutiérrez) — starting from concrete practice among the poor rather than from doctrine applied downward. The 1984 Instruction's critique targeted versions of this method it judged to import Marxist analysis uncritically.

The Argentine 'theology of the people': a current born from the same post-conciliar ferment as liberation theology but centered on popular culture and religiosity rather than class analysis. It formed Jorge Bergoglio — which is why readings of Pope Francis as a 'liberation theologian' are contested; the Cronologia sites keep the labels apart.

A Sufi order — a Muslim initiatic brotherhood under a shaykh. The Maryamiyya is the tariqa founded by the perennialist Frithjof Schuon; the documented membership of Rama Coomaraswamy — sedevacantist author and sometime professor at the SSPX's US seminary — is the reason a Sufi term appears in a Catholic-traditionalism glossary.

The chain of spiritual transmission ('chain, lineage' in Arabic) through which a Sufi order traces the authority of its living shaykh back, master to master, to the Prophet Muhammad — most orders route the chain through Ali, the Naqshbandiyya through Abu Bakr. The silsila functions as a shaykh's credential and source of legitimacy: initiation counts because the initiator himself stands in an unbroken chain. The full style of Schuon's order — Tariqa Shadhiliyya-Darqawiyya-Alawiyya-Maryamiyya — is a silsila compressed into a name.

terms.variants: an Arabic title also used for civil and military officials; in Sufism, the shaykh's authorized representative
terms.definition: In Sufi orders, a disciple (murid) whom the guide — shaykh, pir or murshid — has authorized to assist in teaching the path to other students: a delegated local representative of the master, a usage found in the Tijaniyya, Shadhiliyya, Rahmaniyya and related orders. Outside Sufism the same title has named village intermediaries in medieval India and an officer rank in modern Arab armies. The term recurs in the tariqa project's connection threads, which document who held a muqaddam's authorization, where and when.

terms.variants: khalīfa, 'successor, deputy'; khilafat (the office conferred); cf. caliph
terms.definition: Literally 'successor' or 'deputy' — the word behind 'caliph', the successor of Muhammad at the head of the Muslim community. In Sufi usage it is the disciple a shaykh designates as his deputy or successor: in most cases the shaykh nominates his khalifa during his own lifetime, and the transfer of this khilafat from master to spiritual descendant is what a silsila records link by link. Whether a given khilafat was in fact conferred is often disputed in order histories; the project pages date and attribute such claims.

A Sufi lodge ('corner, nook' in Arabic): the building or complex where an order teaches, houses travelers and holds its dhikr — functioning variously as oratory, school, hostel and shrine, often around a founder's tomb. It is the usual term in North and West Africa; the Persianate world uses khanqah and the Ottoman-Turkish world tekke for the functionally similar institution.

The practice of remembering God by repeating his names or set formulas ('there is no god but God', 'glory be to God'), aloud or silently, alone or in assembly, often counted on beads. Each Sufi order transmits its own dhikr with prescribed posture and breathing, and the collective dhikr session is the characteristic ritual of tariqa life — described by Sufis as a method, alongside meditation (fikr), of striving toward oneness with God.

terms.variants: baya, bay'at; oath of allegiance, Sufi initiation pledge
terms.definition: The Islamic oath of allegiance — from a commercial root, originally the handclasp sealing a bargain — historically given to caliphs and still given to monarchs in Morocco and Saudi Arabia. In Sufism it is the rite of initiation: the seeker's pledge of allegiance to the shaykh of a tariqa, which creates the master–disciple bond and grafts the newcomer onto the order's silsila. In the Cronologia projects, saying someone 'took bay'ah' is therefore a membership claim — initiation, not mere sympathy — and enters a dataset only with self-disclosure or scholarship behind it.

A Latin phrase with four distinct careers, kept apart in the perennialism project's disambiguation. (1) Agostino Steuco coined it (De perenni philosophia, 1540) for the thesis that one God-given wisdom, revealed at the beginning, persists through the ancient philosophers into Christianity. (2) Leibniz took the term over — the first philosopher to use it without crediting Steuco — recasting the primordial truths as seeds to be developed by philosophical progress. (3) Aldous Huxley's The Perennial Philosophy (1945) made it the name of an experiential mystical universalism: a divine Reality accessible in mystical experience across all religions. (4) The Traditionalist School of Guénon and Schuon holds a metaphysical version — a single primordial tradition transmitted only through the orthodox revealed religions — and rejected Huxley-style syncretism. Same phrase, four lineages: a statement about 'perennialism' must say which one it means.

terms.variants: Guénonian Traditionalism; cf. Catholic traditionalism, Evolian 'Traditionalism'
terms.definition: The school of thought stemming from René Guénon, Ananda Coomaraswamy and Frithjof Schuon: perennialism in its metaphysical form — one primordial truth underlying the major world religions in their orthodox forms — joined, in Mark Sedgwick's summary, to the conviction that history is decline rather than progress. Capitalized, the label names this movement only. It is distinct in origin from Catholic traditionalism (attachment to the pre-Vatican II Mass and doctrine — the fsspx project's subject), and distinct again from Julius Evola's politicized 'Traditionalism', the reading later invoked in radical-right politics, whose compatibility with the school scholars dispute. Three different 'traditionalisms': the project pages disambiguate on sight and document the individual bridges between them case by case.
